one_liner: SPCX +7% to $164 — SpaceX's June 12 IPO continues post-listing strength above the $135 offering price, but 11 sessions of data makes technicals unreliable.

price_action.intraday_story: SPCX opened at $157.35, dipped to $151.74 (near the IPO-week support range), then rallied strongly to close at $164.19, just below the $166.17 intraday high. The $12.43 intraday range was wide but the close in the upper quartile is constructive. The $151.74 low held above the prior week's base around $153, maintaining the post-IPO uptrend.

news_catalyst.headline: SpaceX Continues Post-IPO Momentum; Elon Musk Projects $1 Trillion Revenue by 2030
news_catalyst.summary: SpaceX completed the largest IPO in history on June 12 at $135/share, raising $75B. The stock closed around $161 on its first full trading day. Elon Musk has publicly stated SpaceX 'might be able to reach approximately $1 trillion in revenue by 2030,' up from $18.7B in its most recent year, driven by Starlink's 10,000-satellite constellation, launch contracts, and the xAI unit. The stock has settled into a $153–$166 trading range since its debut, supported by analyst targets averaging $187.80. The general AI and space sector tailwind — visible in today's ASTS and RKLB moves — is lifting the entire space ecosystem.

technical.summary:
SPCX has only 11 sessions of trading history — technical indicators are unreliable. RSI and MACD return 0 due to insufficient data. The post-IPO trajectory from $135 to $164 (+21% in 17 days) follows a typical strong-IPO pattern. The $151–$153 range has served as consistent post-IPO support. Volume declining from the first-week peak (126M on June 26) to 79M today is normal post-IPO settlement.

For new IPOs, the key technical levels are the IPO price ($135) as ultimate support, the first-week low as intermediate support (~$148–$151), and the intraday highs as resistance. As more sessions accumulate, traditional technical analysis will become more applicable.

wyckoff_narrative: Too early for Wyckoff analysis — 11 sessions of data don't provide the distribution/accumulation context that Wyckoff methodology requires. The IPO itself is a Form of distribution event (insiders selling to the public), so in a pure Wyckoff framework, post-IPO price action for the first 30–90 days is often best treated as the initial supply/demand discovery phase rather than a classic accumulation or distribution structure.

valuation.summary: DCF not feasible given the extreme growth projections and multiple business units (launch, Starlink, xAI). The $2T+ market cap at current prices implies massive future revenue discounting that cannot be modeled reliably at this stage.

key_strengths: Largest IPO in history — institutional endorsement and massive float
$18.7B in current revenue with Starlink as a growing recurring income stream
Dominant market position: NASA's biggest launch partner
key_concerns: Extremely early-stage public market history — governance and disclosure norms being established
Elon Musk's attention split across Tesla, xAI, and SpaceX creates management risk
Valuation requires believing in $1T revenue by 2030 — an 53x revenue increase
fundamentals.summary: Grade D reflects limited financial history as a public company and extreme valuation multiples. The business is operationally excellent; the valuation requires extraordinary execution.

risks: Post-IPO lock-up expiration: insider selling pressure will increase when the lock-up period ends (typically 180 days post-IPO, around December 2026)
Sector correlation: ASTS and RKLB swings show space stocks are highly correlated on momentum — a sector risk-off event hits all simultaneously
Only 11 sessions of data — technical signals are noise at this stage
Musk governance risk: any negative Tesla/xAI headline creates contagion selling in SPCX
